ATTORNEY AT LAW
9,",", I .g/r*
June 24 | L992
California Fair Political Practices Commission
Scott Hallabrin, Acting ceneral counsel
Blanca M. Breeze, Counsel, Legal Division
428 J Street, Suite 800
2669 ALABAMA AVENUE
ATWATER CALIFORNIA 953OI
TEL. (2O9) 357- 143 r
FAX (2OS) 357-O940
P. O. Box 8O7
Sacramentor CA
Re: Request for Advice
Dear Mr. Hallabrin/Ms, Breeze 3
The Berrenda Iv1esa Water District (District) is a california
Water District 
formed pursuant to Division 13 of the California
Water Code. (A11 references are to the Water Code. ) 
It is a
landowner voting district with each landowner having one vote for
each dollarrs worth of assessed valuation of land to which he holds
titl-e (Section 35003). The District Assessor assesses at1 land
equally for District 
revenue collection purposes, resulting in
voting being directly proportional to land acreage. The District
was formed in 1963 and has been in active operation since 1968.
The Districtrs sole source of water j-s a contractual supply
frorn the California State Water Project. Due to drought conditions
and faiLure of the state to conpfete certain portions of the
Project, the State Water Project has been periodically unable to
supply its contractual commit-ments. It appears +-he state Project
will never be fuiiy completed and thus there lrilI be a perpetual-
over-subscription for the water supply contracted for delivery by
the Proj ect.
The District contains virtually 
no residential properties.
OnIy one landowner fanily Iives in the District and the balance of
the residents are farm ernployees who live in farmer owned housing.
The District is conprised of approximately 55,OOO acres which is
owned by approximately 6O different entities.
The District 
raises its revenue by virtue of ad valorern
assessments, lrater tolls and standby charges. The standby charges
are pursuant to section 35470 and are irnposed on the basis of water
avail-abil-ity whether the $/ater is used or not.
95804-0807


California Fair Political Practices commission
Jvlre 24, 1992
Page 2
The District is governed by a five rnember Board of Directors
elected by l-ando$iners. Two nembers of the board of directors are
enployees of a corporation hlhich o$rns over 17,000 acres in the
Di;trict; 
two othei menbers are ernployees of another corporation
which owns over 12,ooo acres in the District; 
and the fifth 
rnember
of the board is ernployed by a company !'rhich owns over 3,000 acres
in the District. 
ihe balance of the land is comprised of holdings
ranging from 2 to 3600 acres. A11 of the operating farms are large
(300 acres or nore) and the sma11er, unfarned parcels are generaffy
not in the standby charge area.
Due to the rising cost of water and power in the District,
annual crops have ceased to be planted and only permanent crops
(trees and vines) are currently econo:nic to farm-
The Districtrs contractual water entitlement, if avaitable, is
rnore than is necessary to irrj.gate existing crops but because of
the above stated conditions, the District's 
apportioned share of
the state water Entitlement suppLy in the last two years has been
less than was needed to properly irrigate the landownersr permanent
plantlngs.
Some ownersr Lands are planted virtually 
1002 to permanent
crops; some o$rners lands are 1008 open land; and some own a
combination of lands planted to permanent crops and open l-ands.
The owners of the non-planted lands have, in recent years been
at odds with the owners of planted lands over issues such as the
imposition of the standby charge, the construction of groundwater
storage projects, the potential sale of the Districtrs 
excess
contrtctual water entitlement and the foreclosure of properties
delinquent in payment of District taxes and standby charges.
on the standby issue, some o$/ners of the unplanted lands cfaim
that it is econornicatty irnpractical to plant new trees and vines in
the District and $/ant to elininate the acreage standby charge and
to correspondingly increase the rate of the water tolls per acre
foot of witer delivered. The standby charges are incorporated in
the Districtrs by-laws and can only be changed by an election or by
a 4/5ths vote of the board, with approval of the Kern county Board
of Supervisors (Section 35305). Some owners of the Planted lands
assert that the revenue program has been in effect since the
forrnation of the District, 
that standby charges encourage
devefopment thus adding to the financial stability of the District,
that tfre prolIram hras in existence when they purchased their
properties, and they object to any ttwholesafert change of that
program at this time.


California Fair PoIitical Practices Cornmission
June 24, L992
Page 3
on the groundvrater storage projects issue, some o$/ners of
unplanted lands feel that these Projects are only for the benefit
of the planted lands and object to the District incurring further
obligations which are in turn an obLigation on aII District lands.
on the water sale issue, some owners of unplanted lands wish
the District to permanently selI its excess contractual- entitlenent
to an outside entity and distribute a portion of the proceeds to
owners who wish to quit farming and permanently fa1lol^, their Iands.
(water code section 35425 pernits the sale of rrsurplus water not
then necessary for use within the districttt. ) 
some owners of the
planted lands assert that the state water supply has proven to be
unreliable and wish to maintain the excess enti-tlement to assure an
adequate supply in water short years. 
(The District currently
contracts with its parent agency, the Kern county water Agency for
the sale of any excess water allocations, within the county, on a
year to year basis. )
On the foreclosure issue, several thousand acres have been
foreclosed by the District due to the failure of owners to pay
standby charges. several thousand more acres are in default and on
the verge of being forecl-osed. some orrners of the delinquent lands
oppose foreclosure because the debt was incurred due to the standby
charges which they consider to be unfair. 
Sone owners of the
pJ,anted lands assert that foreclosure j-s necessary to enforce the
credibility 
of the standby charges and to protect the financial
integrity of the District and its bond holders.
A11 of these issues are inter-related and have sub-issues and
other related issues and thus at nany, if not nost board rneetings,
one or rnore issues arise which put these factions at odds. The
board of directors appears to be currently split 3-2 on each of
these issues.
There are obviously many millions of dollars at stake on both
sides and the board wishes your advice on whether they nust abstain
fron voting on these issues. f have in the past considered that
the very nature of the District wherein a board nember must be a
landowner (Section 34700), puts each member in a continued
I'conf l-ict of interesti and that the concept underlying Division 13
is that 
landowners controlling powers should be in direct
proportion to the extent of their econonic interests. (Sec. 35003).
we have considered these j-ssues to be those $rhich affect the publ,ic
generally since the vast rnajority of the public are absentee
]andowners with a conmon profit motive involved.
f have considered your Advice Letter No. A-92-077 wherein you
reconmend a rrlotrr method to requalify vrater district directors but


California Fair Pol,itical Practices Conmission
June 24, 1992
Page 4
it appears that this method would bog this District down into chaos
because a different faction could be chosen on inter-related issues
resulting in inconsistent votes. 
Also, if a permanent three
members were chosen by lot, 
it coul-d result in the minority
controlling the affairs of the District for the remainder of their
terms which would be j-nconsistent !'rith the concept of landowner-
value control . 
Atso, if a decision was made on one of these
issues, it could be irrevocable and \^/ould adversely affect the
rna j ority in perpetuity.
PLease give me your advice on whether these issues can be
considered to affect the public generally under Regulation 187o3 or
whether the directors on either or both sides of these issues rnust
di.squalify and requalify thelflselves. Additionally, if they nust
disqualify themselves and a quorum is lacking, can they all
requalify as opposed to using the rtlotrt rnethod?
Please calf me if you need any further inforrnation on the
matter .
u-4.
GGL : elz
cc3 District


9J-:-+q
ATTORNEY AT LAW
9".,r, g ,g/.*
Re: Berrenda
Request
2669 ALABAMA AVENUE
ATWATER, CALIFORNIA 953OI
TEL. (2O9) 357-r43r
FAX (2O9) 357-O940
september 9, 1992
Falr PoliticaI Practices
Scott Hal-l-abrin, Counsel,
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95840
Commiss ion
LegaI Division
Mesa water District
for Advice
Dear Mr. Hallabrin:
This letter is written as a supplement to my Request
for Advice dated June 24, L992.
I urge upon you, the proposition that the normaf
rul-es of diJqual if ication resultj.ng from financial
interests should not apply to landowner-voter districts
in that the very nature of the enabling acts of such
district 
contemplate that those with the l-argest
financial, interests should control, the affairs of the
district.
The second proposition I urge you to endorse is that
the rule of necessity requires voting by all 
board
menbers of landowner-voter districts in the event that a
rirajority of +-he mernbers of the board have a particuJ.ar
financial- interest which disqualifies them fron voting on
certain issues.
1. 
FINANCIAL INTERESTS IN ISSUES AFFECTING ALL
BOARD MEII{BERS SHOULD NOT RESULT IN 
THE
DI SOUALIFICATION OF ANY BOARD MEMBERS
In my previous letter dated June 24, 1992, I set
forth the various statutes pertaining to California Water
Districts. 
There are similar provisions in the other
fandowner-voter district enabling acts and I will not re-
state them here. 
suffice it to say that such acts
specifically contemplate that the larger the financial
interest held by a landowner, the mor6 pofiticaf cfoui
he/she is granted under the voting sys€en provided in
k!
s$


Fair PoIitical Practices Cornmission
September 9, L992
Page 2
such acts. 
For your Commission to rule that the very
j-nterest whj-ch empowers control to the fandowner under
the act also dj-squalifies the landowner frorn voting,
woufd eviscerate the Iega1 foundation of the act and
could result in l-eaderless chaos.
It has been argued that the l-andowner voting scheme
is iI1ogical and even illegal as being in violation of
the Equa] Prctection clause of the United States
constitution. 
Severaf cases, ho$/ever, have upheld this
nethod of Iandowner control of special districts and have
explained the necessity and wisdom of such control in the
process.
In the case of Salyer Land Co. v. Tulare water
Dj-strict, 410 U.S. 719, 35 L.Ed. 559, 93 S.Ct. 1224, t-lTe
court upheld the Landowner voting scheme for California
water storage Districts 
and in so doingT, spoke as
folfows:
"...The California Legi.slature could quite
reasonably have concl,uded that the number of
landowners and owners of sufficient amounts of
acreage $/hose consent $ras necessary to
organize the distrj-ct $/ould not have subjected
their land to the 1j.en of its possibly very
substantial assessments unless they had a
dominant voice in its control. 
Since the
subjection of the ownersr lands to such liens
was the basis by which the district 
sras to
obtain financing, the proposed district had as
a practicai 
matter to attract 
landowner
support. 
Nor, since assessments against
landowners were to be the sole means by which
the expenses of the district were to be paid,
could it be said to be unfair or inequitable
to repose the franchise in lando$/ners but not
residents. Landowners as a class were to bear
the entire burden of the districtrs costs, and
the State could rationally conclude that they,
to the excl-usion of residents, should be
charged with responsibility for its operation.
we conclude, therefore, that nothing in the
Equa1 Protection clause precluded California
from liniting 
the voting for directors of
appell,ee district 
by totally excluding those
who merely reside within the district.'r
(Enphasis added)


Fair PoIi-ticat Practices Commission
september 9, L992
Page 3
In further justifying the refusal to a1lohI non-
or^rners to vote, the court said:
ItFinally, we note that california has not
left 
the lessee without remedy for his
disenfranchised state. 
Sections 4Lo02 and
41005 of the california water Code provide for
voting in the general etection by proxy. 
To
the extent that a lessee enterj-ng into a lease
of substantial duration, thereby likening his
status more to that of a landowner, feels that
the right to vote in the election of directors
of the district 
is of sufficient inport to
hirn, he may bargain for that right at the tirne
he negotiates his lease. And the longer the
term of the lease, and the nore the j-nterest
of the lessee becomes akin to that of the
Iandowner, presumably the more willing 
the
Iessor will be to assign his right. 
Just as
the Lessee may by contract be required to
reirnburse the 
lessor for 
the district
assessnents so he rnay by contract acquire the
right to vote for district directors.rt
Under these circumstances, the exclusion
of lessees from voting in general elections
for the directors of the district 
does not
violate the Equal Protection Clause.rr
The later case of Ball v. Janes 451 U.S. 1811,
l-ol- S.ct 356, involved an Arizona Agricultural and Power
District. 
In upholding lanCowner voter cont-rcl based on
size of holdings, the court said:
rt...We conclude that the voting scheme
for the District is constitutional because i.t
bears a reasonable relationship 
to 
its
statutory objectives. Here, according to the
stipulation of the parties, the subscriptions
of Iand which rnade the Association and then
the District possible might well have never
occurred had not the subscribing landowners
been assured a special voice in the conduct of
the Distri.ctt s business. rt
rr. . .Moreover, Arizona could rationally
make the weight of their vote dependent upon
the number of acres they own, since that


Fair Political- Practices commission
september 9, L992
Paqe 4
number reasonably reflects the relative risks
they 
incurred 
as 
landowners and the
distribution of the benefits and the burdens
of the District's water operat5.ons. "
In sunmary, it 
is clear that the california
Legislature and the United States Supreme court both
heartily endorse the concept of lando!,/ner control- based
upon the size of holdj,ngs. For this cornnission to rule
that the very qualifj.cations which grant control al-so
disqualifies one fron voting would be indeed incongruous.
2. 
DISOUALIFICATION OF A MAJORITY SHOULD RESULT
rN RE-OUALIFICATION OF ALL
It is not our position that no land-owner voter
should never be disqualified. 
For example, if a project
were buiLt to benefit only a portion of the District, the
directors owning land in that area shoul-d be disqualified
from voting. 
lf, 
ho$/ever, a najority 
were so
disqualified, it is our position that the entire board
shoula be re-qualified under the rrrule of necessityrr as
opposed to requalification of a mere najority under the
rrfotrr method suggested by your predecessors.
In the case of ol-son v. corv (1980) ' 27 cal.3d 532,
the California Supreme court $ras confronted with a case
which affected the salaries of every nember of the court.
Instead of requalifying a quorum by Iot, the court stated
that the whole court was qualified and spoke as follows:
rrThe rlrLe of necessity provides that a
judge is not disqualified frour adjudicating a
cause because of a personaf financial interest
if there is no other judge or court availabl-e
to hear and resoLve the cause.rl
In 1 Am Jur 2d Adninistrative Law 566, the rule is
stated as foLlows:
rrThere is 
an exception, based upon
necessity, to the rule of disqualification of
an administratj-ve officer. 
D isqual if icat ion
will not be pennitted to destroy the onLy
tribunal with pohrer in the prernises. 
A;
officer, 
otherwise disqualified, 
nay stil1
act, if his failure to act would nec6ssarily
result in a failure of justice.


Fair PoIitical Practices Cornmission
september 9, t992
Page 5
rrThus where the statute cl-earIy requires a
hearing before a designated adrninistrative
officer, 
and no other officer can hold the
hearing, then the language of the statute may
not be disregarded nor the legislative intent
defeated by holding that the officer 
is
disqualified. rl
The case of Atkins v. United states 556 F2d La2a
(L977) invotved the contention by a judge that judges
were not subject to income tax increases during their
term of office.
In holding that the entire appellate panel was
quatified to vote, the court spoke as follows:
tt. . .If 
the 
judges of 
this 
court 
are
disqualified 
because of 
their 
indirect
interest in the subject rnatter in controversy
or because of any other interest that could be
substantially affected by the outcome of the
proceedings, at1 of the other judges of the
united States are sinilarly 
disqualified.
There is no federal judge e/ho h/ould be
available to try the cases. Situations of
this kind caused a doctrine known as the rule
of necessity to be developed in the law. rl
rrThe rule of necessity was a part of the
English connon law and has been traced back to
1430 and the Year Books. 
Dimes v. Grand
Junction canaT co., 10 Eng. Rep, 301, 3L3
(1852). 
See a7so, The Independence of the
Judiciary,34 can.B.Rev. 769, 790 (1956); and
Frank, DisquaTitication of Judges, 56 Yale
L.J. 605, 609-L0 (t947). 
The rule, sinply
stated, means that a judge is not disgualified
to try a case because of his personal interest
in the rnatter at issue if there is no other
judge available to hear and decide the case.rl
rrAnother statute, 
28 U.S.C. 5455,
provides that a judge shall disqualify hinsetf
in any case in which he has a substantial
interest 
or 
his 
impartiality 
might 
be
questioned. ordinarily when a judge is naned
as a defendant in a suit brought by a


Fair Political- Practices Commission
September 9, L992
Page 6
defendant before him in tria1, 
such judge
should autornatically disqualify hinself .
However, necessity nay obviate this rul-e
when virtually no judge would be available to
hear the suit because aII federal judges are
co-defendants . rr
xwith respect to disqual i f ication in
civiL actions where the trial 
judge to which
the case happens to be assigned is afso a
defendant in the sane action. 
28 u.S. c.A.
5455 would require that the judge disqualify
himself. 
This court notes, however, that
there is a maxin of 1aw to the effect that
where all 
are disqualified, 
none are
disqualified. 
Evans v. Gore, 253 U.S. 245, 40
s.ct. 550, 64 L.Ed.887.rl
rr. . .Nevertheless we realize 
that 
the
plaintiffs 
are entitled to have their cases
heard and decided by a court of the United
states, and under the lai^, there is no other
court to which they coul-d 90. 
should we
refuse to hear and decide their cases, the
doors of the courts would be closed to thern.
This could anount to a denial of due process
under the 14th amendment to the Constitution.rl
The case of Brinkley v. Hassicr 82 F2d 351 involved
the revocation of a doctorts license by a medical board
whose members had a1l been exposed to prejudicial pre-
hearing publicity. 
In holding that the board was not
disqualified. 
The court spoke as follows:
rrFrom the very necessity of the case has
grown the rule that disqualif ication witt not
be permitted to destroy the only tribunal with
power in the premj-ses. ff the 1aw provides
for a substitution of personnel on a board or
court, or if another tribunal exists to which
resort may be had, a disqualified rnernber may
not act. But $ihere no such provision is made,
the 1aw cannot be nultified or the doors to
justice 
barred because of prejudice or
disqualification of a rnernber of L cLurt or an
adninistrative tribunal . I


Fair PoIitical Practices commission
September 9, L992
Page 7
In sunmary, where a majority of the members of a
l-andowner-voter district 
are disqualified because of
particular interests in a particular project, the entire
board should be allowed to vote under the rrrule of
necessityrt endorsed by the above authorities.
To hold otherwise $/ould deprive the landolrners of
the very control granted to them by the Legislature and
endorsed by the Unr-ted States Suprene Court.
It could also result in extremely unacceptabl-e
circumstances. For instance, as alluded to in ny
previous l-etter, one set of directors could vote one
month to construct a nulti-nillion 
dollar storage or
transmj-ssion facility 
and another set of directors could
vote the next month to se1l a rnajor portion of the
Districtts 
water entitlement, thus rendering the
improvernents unneccessary or useless.
It is respectfully requested that you grant a letter
of advice holding:
1. 
That in instances of financial 
interests
affecting an entire landowner-voter district,
the mernbers of the board are not disqualified
fron voting; and
2. 
that in instances where a majority of 
a
Landowner-voter 
district 
board 
are
disqualified due to a particular financial
interest, 
the entire board is requalified
under the rtrul-e of necessity. tl
GGL: elz
-D i str ict
-Ernest Conant, Atty.
-Association of california
Attn: Connie Barber
CC:
Water Agencies

month to construct a nulti-nillion 
dollar storage or
transmj-ssion facility 
and another set of directors could
vote the next month to se1l a rnajor portion of the
Districtts 
water entitlement, thus rendering the
improvernents unneccessary or useless.

That in instances of financial 
interests
affecting an entire landowner-voter district,
the mernbers of the board are not disqualified
fron voting; and

that in instances where a majority of 
a
Landowner-voter
